The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

[[ja:昌平坂学問所]]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

[[ja:%EC%87%BC%ED%97%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%9E%90%EC%B9%B4 %ED%95%99%EB%AC%B8%EC%86%8C]] [[ja:昌平坂学問所]]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

[[ja:昌平坂学問所]]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

[[uk:Академія Сьохейдзака]] [[ru:Академия Сёхэйдзака]] [[ja:%EC%87%BC%ED%97%A4%EC%9D%B4%EC%9E%90%EC%B9%B4 %ED%95%99%EB%AC%B8%EC%86%8C]] [[ja:昌平坂学問所]]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:completion tier 2]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|儒学者}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|儒学者}}" to "|1=Ru (Confucian)|儒学者|zh|儒生}}"
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]

revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Attempting to force efresh]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}}== See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}} == See Also! == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index National Cultural Property Database] * [http://www.seido.or.jp/ Yushima Seido (Shibunkai Foundation)] * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/education/kindaikyoiku.htm The Origin of Modern Education]
{{Tokyo University's Historical Precursors}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605年}} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630年}} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790年}} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870年}} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}} == See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year }} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870th year }} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}} == See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year }} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870th year }} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}} == See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}

'''Shōheizaka Academy''' (昌平坂学問所):
The {{ill|Shōheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所}} was an educational and research institution directly managed by the {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}. Established in {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2) in {{ill|Yushima|ja|湯島}}, Kanda, it served as a centralized location for Confucian studies. Its formal designation was "Gakumonjo" (学問所), but it was also referred to as "Shōheikō" (昌平黌).
== History == [[File:Yushima Seido6.jpg|thumb|The "Taiseiden" (Confucius Shrine) at Yushima Seido (February 3, 2010)]] {{See also|Yushima Seido}} === As the Hayashi Family's Private Academy === In {{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }} (Keichō 10), {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} entered the service of Tokugawa Ieyasu as a Buddhist scholar. By {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year }} (Kan'ei 7), Tokugawa Iemitsu granted the Hayashi family land in Ueno for a school. This was the foundation of what would later become the Shōheizaka Academy.
=== Establishment of the Gakumonjo === In {{ill|1790|ja|1790th year }} (Kansei 2), as part of the "Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism" (寛政異学の禁), the institution was reorganized into a government-operated academy.
=== Transition to "Shōhei School" === During the Meiji Restoration, the academy was briefly rebranded as the "Shōhei School" but was eventually dissolved in {{ill|1870|ja|1870th year }} (Meiji 3). It had limited influence on the formation of the modern {{ill|University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=1 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=1998-12 |isbn=9784885942785}} * {{Cite book|和書 |title=昌平坂学問所日記 |volume=2 |editor=斯文会・橋本昭彦 |publisher=斯文会・東洋書院（発売） |date=2002-03 |isbn=9784885943188}} == See Also == * {{ill|Confucian Scholars|ja|1=Ru (Confucian scholar)|儒学者|zh|儒生}} * {{ill|Hayashi Razan|ja|林羅山}} * {{ill|Kansei Edict on Neo-Confucianism|ja|寛政異学の禁}}
